reasoning: 43.1 AdjEM gap is insurmountable. Auburn's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) confirms elite ceiling. Alabama State's best twin (2012 Norfolk State) pulled a 15-over-2 upset — wrong seed line to project here.

Only 2.3 AdjEM gap favoring Louisville. Creighton's top archetype is 2014 Connecticut (Champion, 0.932 similarity) — that UConn team was similarly underseeded and went on a title run. Louisville's twin (2018 Michigan, Runner-Up) is strong, but Creighton's offensive efficiency (AdjO 118.4) at a controlled tempo gives them the edge in this coin-flip.

Michigan's 6.3 AdjEM edge holds. Their archetype (2014 Michigan State, Final Four) suggests deep tournament capacity. UC San Diego's best twin (2010 Tennessee, Sweet 16) reached the second weekend, making this closer than pure efficiency suggests, but Michigan's overall profile wins.

11.9 AdjEM gap and Texas A&M's archetype (2012 Syracuse, Elite Eight) both point to advancement. Yale's twin (2022 Michigan, Sweet 16) had a stronger profile than this Yale team. The physicality mismatch is decisive.

Narrow 2.7 AdjEM edge for Ole Miss. Both archetypes are strong — Ole Miss matches 2018 Michigan (Runner-Up) and UNC matches 2011 Connecticut (Champion in some twins). Ole Miss's defensive edge (AdjD 97.0 vs 99.8) decides a close 6-11.

20.3 AdjEM gap is overwhelming. Iowa State's archetype (2013 Duke) lost in R64, which is a caution flag, but a 3-14 with this efficiency gap is a 95%+ favorite regardless. Lipscomb's twin (2013 Harvard) also lost in R64.

Only 2.6 AdjEM edge, but Marquette's champion archetype (2014 Connecticut, 0.963 similarity) is remarkable. That UConn team won the title from a similar efficiency profile. New Mexico's twin (2010 Baylor, Sweet 16) is solid but lower ceiling.

24.5 AdjEM gap. Michigan State's archetype (2010 Duke, Champion) reflects elite tournament DNA. Bryant's twin (2012 Norfolk State) pulled an upset, but that was a 15-over-2 — the stats don't support a repeat here against MSU's superior defense.
46.0 AdjEM gap — the largest in the tournament. Duke's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) and AdjO of 130.6 suggest historically elite offense. Mount St. Mary's has no path.

Baylor's 1.8 AdjEM edge and archetype (2013 Syracuse, Final Four) give them the slight nod. Mississippi State's twin (2014 Connecticut, Champion) is impressive, but Baylor's offensive firepower (AdjO 121.3) and Scott Drew's tournament experience tilt this 8-9.

7.2 AdjEM advantage. Oregon's archetype (2014 Connecticut, Champion, 0.960) is extremely bullish. Liberty's twin (2016 Syracuse, Final Four) pulled an upset from the 10-seed line, not the 12. Oregon's efficiency edge holds.

20.9 AdjEM gap. Arizona's archetype (2016 Villanova, Champion, 0.934) suggests title-contender profile. Akron's twin (2012 Ohio, Sweet 16) was a 13-seed upset specialist, but Arizona's offensive firepower (AdjO 124.9) neutralizes that risk.

7.3 AdjEM edge driven by BYU's elite offense (AdjO 126.8). BYU's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up, 0.870) matches a team that reached the championship game. VCU's twin (2013 Syracuse, Final Four) has a strong profile but lower offensive output.

23.5 AdjEM gap. Wisconsin's archetype (2018 Michigan, Runner-Up, 0.931) matches a team that went deep. Montana's twin (2013 Harvard) was a first-round exit. Wisconsin's defensive profile (AdjD 96.8) suffocates mid-majors.

3.7 AdjEM edge with elite defense (AdjD 93.0 — 8 points better than Vanderbilt's 101.1). Saint Mary's archetype (2014 Wisconsin, Final Four) was similarly defense-first and slow-tempo. Vanderbilt's twin (2022 Iowa State, Sweet 16) is good but defense wins here.

29.0 AdjEM gap. Alabama's archetype (2012 North Carolina, Elite Eight) reflects high-powered offense that dominates in tournament settings. Robert Morris's twin (2012 Norfolk State) was a 15-seed upset specialist, but Alabama's AdjO 127.7 at 75.1 tempo overwhelms.
42.2 AdjEM gap with the nation's best defense (AdjD 87.3). Houston's archetype (2019 Virginia, Champion) won the title with a similarly suffocating defensive style. SIU Edwardsville has no path.

9.3 AdjEM gap is enormous for an 8-9 matchup — Gonzaga is massively underseeded. Gonzaga's archetype (2016 Villanova, Champion, 0.946) is a title-winner profile. Georgia's twin (2013 Syracuse, Final Four) is solid but Gonzaga's offensive arsenal (AdjO 124.8) is too much.

7.6 AdjEM edge. Clemson's archetype (2013 Syracuse, Final Four) suggests a team capable of a deep run. McNeese's twin (2016 Syracuse, Final Four) is intriguing for a 12-seed, but Clemson's defensive discipline (AdjD 95.5 at 64.6 tempo) grinds out mid-major challengers.

16.7 AdjEM gap. Purdue's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) is literally the same program reaching back-to-back title games. High Point's twin (2023 Furman, R32) had an upset pedigree, but not against this level of offensive firepower (AdjO 125.6).

6.1 AdjEM edge. Illinois's archetype (2016 Oklahoma, Final Four) reflects a team that can make a deep run from this seed line. Xavier's twin (2021 UCLA, Final Four) is impressive but from a different seed line. Illinois's offense (AdjO 122.5) at a fast tempo controls this game.

18.7 AdjEM gap. Kentucky's archetype (2016 Oklahoma, Final Four, 0.931) and Troy's twin (2022 Saint Peter's, Elite Eight) create an interesting dynamic — Troy's archetype was a massive upset specialist. But the efficiency gap is too wide for a 14-seed.

7.8 AdjEM advantage. UCLA's archetype (2012 Syracuse, Elite Eight, 0.971) is a near-perfect match with a team that went deep. Utah State's twin (2022 Michigan, Sweet 16) suggests second-weekend capacity, but UCLA's defense (AdjD 94.3) is the difference.

26.2 AdjEM gap with elite defense (AdjD 91.9). Tennessee's archetype (2015 Wisconsin, Runner-Up, 0.979 — near perfect similarity) reached the championship game with the same defensive identity. Wofford has no path against this wall.

36.5 AdjEM gap. Florida's archetype (2017 Gonzaga, Runner-Up, 0.920) reached the title game with a similar offensive-juggernaut profile. Norfolk State's twin (2012 Norfolk State, R32) was a 15-seed upset team, not a 16-seed one. Florida cruises.

3.6 AdjEM edge. UConn's archetype (2013 Syracuse, Final Four) and back-to-back championship DNA give them a slight edge in this 8-9. Oklahoma's twin (2021 UCLA, Final Four, 0.927) is strong, but UConn's offensive efficiency (AdjO 120.8) in the half-court decides it.

Colorado State has a 5.6 AdjEM ADVANTAGE over the 5-seed Memphis (18.4 vs 12.8) — one of the biggest misseeds in the bracket. CSU's archetype (2013 Syracuse, Final Four, 0.937) matches a team that went deep. Memphis's twin (2017 South Carolina, Final Four) had a magic run but worse numbers. The data says upset.

18.8 AdjEM gap. Maryland's archetype (2011 Duke, Sweet 16, 0.986 — near-perfect) is a methodical 4-seed profile. Grand Canyon's twin (2014 Mercer, R32) had upset potential from the 14-line, not the 13. Maryland's defense (AdjD 92.1) is elite.

9.3 AdjEM edge. Missouri's archetype (2016 Oklahoma, Final Four) reflects a high-powered offensive team with deep run capability. Drake's twin (2010 Butler, Runner-Up) had incredible tournament magic, but Drake plays at 59.1 tempo — too slow to generate comeback possessions.

22.8 AdjEM gap. Texas Tech's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up, 0.924) is a near-match for a team that reached the championship. UNC Wilmington's twin (2013 Harvard, R64) lost in the first round. Texas Tech's offense (AdjO 126.1) is overwhelming.

4.1 AdjEM edge. Kansas's archetype (2010 West Virginia, Final Four, 0.947) and defensive profile (AdjD 93.7 vs 95.5) give them the edge. Arkansas's twin (2015 Michigan State, Final Four) has deep-run DNA, making this closer than the seed line suggests.

19.9 AdjEM gap with the tournament's best defense (AdjD 90.0). St. John's archetype (2010 West Virginia, Final Four) matches a team that made a deep run. Omaha's twin (2013 Harvard, R64) was a first-round exit. This is a blowout.
